working: At T=0, N1 sends messages simultaneously to N2 and N3.
N3 forwards after a 1-step delay, so its messages reach N4 and N5 at T=1.
N2 forwards after a 2-step delay, so its message reaches N4 at T=2.
N4 requires messages from both N2 and N3, so it activates when the second one arrives: T=2.
N5 activates immediately upon receiving any message; it first receives from N3 at T=1.
When N4 activates at T=2, it also sends to N5 immediately, but this is later than N5's first activation.

Without N3's message, N4 never gets both required inputs, so N4 never activates.
Without N3->N5 and without N4 activation, N5 never receives any message, so N5 never activates.
Counterfactual with N2 delay = 0: N2's message reaches N4 at T=0, N3's reaches N4 at T=1.
N4 activates when the second required message arrives, so in that case at T=1.

Now T3 is released at 3 and can run on M1 from 3 to 7.
T6 is released at 3 and can run on M2 from 3 to 6.
T5 requires both T3 and T4, so earliest start is max(7,3)=7. Put T5 on M1 from 8 to 10 or on M2 from 7 to 9 if machine availability allows.

Using M1 from 8 to 10 with M1 idle 7 to 8 gives completion 10; a cleaner optimal variant is M1: T1 0-3, T3 3-7; M2: T2 0-2, T4 2-3, T6 3-6, idle 6-7, and T5 7-9, which would actually complete by 9 if T5 is on M2.
But that 9-hour variant is feasible: T5 depends only on T3 and T4, both done by 7 and 3 respectively, and M2 is free at 6. So T5 can run 7-9 on M2.
Therefore the true minimum makespan is 9 hours.
An optimal schedule is: M1: T1 0-3, T3 3-7. M2: T2 0-2, T4 2-3, T6 3-6, T5 7-9. All dependencies and machine constraints are satisfied.
